source_texts: Next, Nana tackled Steiner without disgust, but also without love. She called him a dirty Jew. She seemed to be gratifying an old hatred which she could not very well explain to herself. He was fat, he was stupid, and she turned him about, taking double mouthfuls, wishing to have done with the Prussian all the quicker. He had given up Simone. His Bosphorus speculation was already in jeopardy. Nana hastened his downfall by the most lavish expenditure. For a month still he struggled, performing miracles. He covered Europe with a colossal publicity—posters, advertisements, prospectuses—and extracted money from the most distant countries. All those savings, the louis of the speculators the same as the sous of the poor people, were swallowed up in the Avenue de Villiers. He had also gone into partnership with an iron-founder in Alsace. There were there, in a corner of the country, workmen black with coal dust, bathed with perspiration, who, night and day, tightened their muscles and heard their bones crack, to supply the means for Nana’s pleasures. She devoured all like a great fire—the thefts at the Bourse, the earnings of labour. This time she finished Steiner. She returned him to the pavement, sucked to the bone, so emptied that he remained even incapable of inventing a fresh roguery. In the collapse of his banking establishment he went crazy; he trembled at the name of the police. He was made a bankrupt; and the mere word “money” bewildered him, threw him into a childish state of embarrassment—he who had handled millions. One evening when with her, he burst out crying. He asked her to lend him a hundred francs to pay his servant; and Nana, affected and amused by this ending of the terrible old man who for twenty years past had been skimming the Paris market, brought him the money, saying,

The wonderful thing about the collision between young minds is that you can never predict the spark or anticipate the flash. What will the next moment bring? No one knows. The burst of laughter succeeds compassion. In the comic instant seriousness comes into play. Impulses are prompted by a chance word. Each individual’s verve is paramount. A joke is enough to create an opening for the unexpected. These are exchanges that take abrupt turns, with sudden realignments of perspective. Fortuitousness is the scene-shifter in such conversations.

“Wait! Wait! Let’s talk of this later. You can give this olai to the Emperor personally now. Later, if the poets come, they will go on talking…” “You there! Take this young soldier to the Emperor’ss chamber,” he said to a soldier.

“They’re coming, here they are. And the General is making up the rear. I’ll fling open all the doors and call all my daughters, every single one, now, this minute,” Lebedev mumbled softly in a fluster, dashing from door to door.

The torso of a capripede caryatid. A carriage drawn by a pair of steeds flew up to the carriage porch, and a lackey, tricorne on his head, gave the handle a click, and the door flew open, flinging back a coat of arms adorned with tiny crowns (a unicorn goring a knight). Responding to greetings, a hand invested in leather touched the brim of a top hat.
“Thandavaraya, I don’t think this is a real horse. It may belong to the poi kaal kudirai (false horse performance) group,” said one of the soldiers. “Fine then.

A thousand lords with girdle-jade walk in front and rear. The many flags of guardsmen stand both left and right. Those holding gilt bludgeons, And halberds and axes, March in pairs and pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity. The dragons fly and the phoenixes dance; The falcons soar and the eagles take wing. This Son of Heaven’s an upright sage; The righteous ministers are good. They increase our bliss by a thousand years, surpassing Yu and Shun; They secure peace of ten thousand ages, rivaling Yao and Tang. We also see the curve-handled umbrella, And robes with rolling dragons— Their glare lighting up each other; The jade joined-rings, The phoenix fans, Waving through holy mist. Those caps of pearls and belts of jade; The purple sashes and medals of gold. A thousand rows of soldiers protect the Throne; Two lines of marshals uphold the carriage. This emperor, cleansed and sincere, bows to the Buddha, Glad to raise incense and seek virtue’s fruit. The grand cortege of the Tang emperor soon arrived in front of the temple. The emperor ordered a halt to the music, left the carriages, and led many officials in the worship of Buddha by taking up burning incense sticks in their hands. After bowing three times holding the incense, they raised their heads and looked around them. This was indeed a splendid religious hall. You see Dancing flags and banners; Bright, gleaming sunshades. Dancing flags and banners Fill the air with strands of flashing colored mists. Bright, gleaming sunshades Glow in the sun as fiery bolts. Imposing, the gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha;14 Most awesome, the jade features of the arhats. Divine flowers fill the vases. Sandalwood incense burn in the urns. The divine flowers filling the vases Adorn the temple with a brilliant forest of brocade. The sandalwood incense burning in the urns Covers the clear sky with waves of fragrant clouds. Piled high on red trays are fruits in season. On colored counters, mounds of cakes and sweets rest. Rows of noble priests chant the holy sūtras To save from their travails those orphaned souls. Taizong and his officials each lifted the incense; they also worshipped the golden body of the Buddha and paid homage to the arhats. Thereafter, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the various monks to greet the Tang emperor. After the ceremony, they went back to their seats according to their rank and station. The priest then presented Taizong with the proclamation for the deliverance of the orphaned souls. It read: The supreme virtue is vast and endless, for Buddhism is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The spirit of the pure and the clean circulates freely and flows everywhere in the Three Regions. There are a thousand changes and ten thousand transformations, all regulated by the forces of yin and yang. Boundless and vast indeed are the substance, the function, the true nature, and the permanence of such phenomena. But look at those orphaned souls, how worthy they are of our pity and commiseration! Now by the holy command of Taizong, we have selected and assembled various priests, who will engage in meditation and in the proclamation of the Law. Flinging wide the gates of salvation and setting in motion many vessels of mercy, we would deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Woe and save you from perdition and from the Sixfold Path. You will be led to return to the way of truth and to enjoy the bliss of Heaven. Whether it be by motion, rest, or nonactivity, you will be united with, and become, pure essences. Therefore make use of this noble occasion, for you are invited to the pleasures of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass so that you may find release from Hell’s confinement, ascend quickly and freely to ultimate bliss, and travel without restraint in the Region of the West.15 The poem says: An urn of immortal incense. Some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, Receive now Heaven’s endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, You lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed With peace long and all-embracing. Highly pleased by what he read, Taizong said to the monks, “Be firm, all of you, in your devotion, and do not slack in your service to Buddha. After the achievement of merit and after each has received his blessing, we shall reward you handsomely. Be assured that you will not have labored in vain.”

He told them. But we shall not repeat it here. He told the story briefly and impersonally. He did not speak of his love and his feelings; he told them only that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself “in view of certain new developments.” He did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions anymore, did not go into subtleties. Besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that this was not what interested them most.

All six tiny little rooms were steam heated, as a result of which you were suffocated by moist heat in the apartment. The window panes sweated; the visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofia Petrovna Likhutina herself was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum. How could there possibly be any perspective?

“Ah!” he exclaimed, interrupting himself. “There come Mignon and Steiner! They are always together. You know that Steiner begins to have had enough of Rose; so the husband sticks to him like a plaster lest he should escape.”
rationales: Outputs 2 and 3 share multiple distinctive features: both end with an incomplete sentence concluding with 'saying,' (no closing quote or content), both describe Steiner's mental breakdown with the police/bankruptcy sequence in the same order, both use 'childlike state of confusion/embarrassment,' both describe Nana as 'moved and amused' by the downfall, both use 'deep-seated animosity/hatred she couldn't quite articulate,' and both describe the workers as 'coal-dusted' and toiling to finance Nana's lifestyle. The structural template across both outputs is nearly identical, with only surface-level word substitutions differentiating them.

Outputs 0 and 1 share nearly identical stylistic fingerprints: both preserve the original's staccato, aphoristic sentence structure (short declarative sentences in sequence), both use archaic/formal register, both maintain the same ordering of ideas (spark/flash → laughter/tenderness → jest/serious → chance word → sovereign spirit → jest opens the unexpected → abrupt turns → chance as stage-manager/director), and both use very similar vocabulary ('jest'/'buffoonery', 'stage-manager'/'director', 'foresee the spark'/'foresee the spark'). The only differences are minor word substitutions, which is consistent with two outputs from the same paraphraser with slight variation.

Outputs 1 and 4 both open with 'Hold on!', use similar phrasing like 'discuss this later/another time', mention the poets as a contextual reason, and end with a nearly identical instruction to a nearby soldier/guard to escort the young soldier to the Emperor. Their sentence rhythm, modernized tone, and structural choices are consistently parallel, indicating they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both open with 'They're coming, sir, they're coming,' both include 'And even the general is coming after them,' both use 'now, right now,' and both describe Lebedev whispering fearfully while waving his arms and moving between doors. The only minor differences are slight word choices like 'lunging' vs 'dashing' and 'the other' vs 'the next,' which are typical of minor variation within the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same stylistic fingerprints: both begin with the caryatid/goat-body description, both use 'three-cornered hat,' both describe 'a unicorn impaling a knight,' and both describe a 'gloved hand' touching 'the brim of a top hat' in response to greetings. Their sentence structures, vocabulary choices, and overall tone are consistently parallel, distinguishing them from the more archaic/literary style of outputs 2 and 3, and the slightly different phrasing of output 0.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same core framing — a soldier doubting the horse's authenticity by suggesting it belongs to a troupe/group that performs with prop or fake horses. Both use dialogue format with similar attribution and focus on the 'performing troupe' concept rather than the temple festival context used in outputs 0 and 1. Output 4 appears to be a more elaborated version of output 3's interpretation, with 'poikal kudiraiyar group' paraphrased as 'troupe that performs with prop horses,' indicating the same underlying paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 both display the same distinctive short, poetic line structure with parallel construction. Both reference the same ancient Chinese rulers (Yu, Shun, Yao, Tang) in comparative phrases about surpassing legendary rulers. Both describe ceremonial royal procession elements in terse verse-like lines. They share the same stylistic fingerprint of minimal, fragmented poetic paraphrase with similar imagery (flags, ceremonial objects, mythical creatures soaring), suggesting they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprints: both use the exact phrase 'concise and detached,' both refer to 'recent changes' as the cited reason, both use similar phrasing about not elaborating on motivations or nuances, and both frame the interrogators' lack of questions in nearly identical ways. These parallel word choices and structural patterns strongly suggest they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same literary register, near-identical sentence structures, both preserve the 'warm dew on a chrysanthemum' simile faithfully, list the same sweating entities (window panes, visitors, maid, husband, Sofia Petrovna), and both include the rhetorical question about 'perspective.' The other outputs either omit the chrysanthemum metaphor (0, 2) or restructure it significantly (1). The closeness in vocabulary, syntax, and preserved source details strongly indicates they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing: both use 'blurted out,' both have the exact sentence 'Here come Mignon and Steiner! They're inseparable,' and both use the 'clinging to him like glue' metaphor. The only minor differences are 'breaking off his thought' vs 'cutting himself off' and 'make sure he doesn't get away' vs 'prevent him from leaving,' which are typical of slight variation within the same paraphrasing system.

human_par3: Then Nana took up with Steiner without disgust but without love. She called him a dirty Jew; she seemed to be paying back an old grudge, of which she had no distinct recollection. He was fat; he was stupid, and she got him down and took two bites at a time in order the quicker to do for this Prussian. As for him, he had thrown Simonne over. His Bosphorous scheme was getting shaky, and Nana hastened the downfall by wild expenses. For a month he struggled on, doing miracles of finance. He filled Europe with posters, advertisements and prospectuses of a colossal scheme and obtained money from the most distant climes. All these savings, the pounds of speculators and the pence of the poor, were swallowed up in the Avenue de Villiers. Again he was partner in an ironworks in Alsace, where in a small provincial town workmen, blackened with coal dust and soaked with sweat, day and night strained their sinews and heard their bones crack to satisfy Nana's pleasures. Like a huge fire she devoured all the fruits of stock-exchange swindling and the profits of labor. This time she did for Steiner; she brought him to the ground, sucked him dry to the core, left him so cleaned out that he was unable to invent a new roguery. When his bank failed he stammered and trembled at the idea of prosecution. His bankruptcy had just been published, and the simple mention of money flurried him and threw him into a childish embarrassment. And this was he who had played with millions. One evening at Nana's he began to cry and asked her for a loan of a hundred francs wherewith to pay his maidservant. And Nana, much affected and amused at the end of this terrible old man who had squeezed Paris for twenty years, brought it to him and said:
llama_31_8b: Nana confronted Steiner without revulsion, yet without affection. She referred to him as a dirty Jew. She seemed to be satisfying a deep-seated hatred that she couldn't fully understand herself. She perceived him as obese and incompetent, and roughly handled him, hoping to hasten his demise. He had lost Simone and was already on shaky ground due to his Bosphorus speculation. Nana accelerated his downfall by extravagantly overspending his resources. For another month, Steiner fought back, miraculously achieving success. He blanketed Europe with a massive publicity campaign and drew money from remote countries. Nonetheless, all those collected funds, whether from wealthy investors or poor people, were fed into Nana's lavish spending habits in the Avenue de Villiers. Additionally, he had formed a partnership with a metalworker in Alsace. There, in a remote corner of the country, laborers exhausted from their work were tasked with toiling night and day to generate income, which ultimately fueled Nana's extravagance. She exploited it all like a hungry fire, consuming financial losses and worker earnings with reckless abandon. This time, she left Steiner financially crushed. She had taken away everything, leaving him penniless and so desperate that he couldn't even think of a new scheme to dupe others. In the collapse of his business empire, Steiner went insane, becoming terrified at the prospect of police involvement. He was declared bankrupt, and even the idea of money now flustered him, reducing him to a childlike state of embarrassment. He had once handled millions with ease, but now he was at a loss. One evening, in Nana's presence, Steiner unexpectedly broke down in tears and asked for a hundred francs to pay his servant. Nana, affected and entertained by the reversal of this cunning entrepreneur who had efficiently drained Paris's market for twenty years, provided him with the money, saying.

Nana confronted Steiner withoutKalooking at him with disgust or affection. Instead, she -taunted him with insults, calling him a derogatory name. It seemed that she was feeding a long-standing hatred that she couldn't quite articulate. She mocked his physical appearance and perpetuated his downfalls. Steiner was involved in a failing Bosphorus speculation, and Nana accelerated its demise by overspending lavishly. Despite his initial successes, his empire unraveled as investors from across Europe sought to recover their losses. Those from all walks of life - the wealthy as well as the poor - were left ruined, their savings and hard-earned earnings lost in Nana's extravagant lifestyle. She had also formed a partnership with an ironfounder in Alsace, where workers toiled tirelessly to generate funds for her indulgences. Nana left a trail of devastation as she consumed everything in her path, including stolen funds and the earnings of those who worked hard for their livelihood. Steiner was left a hollow shell, unable to come up with new schemes. His banking establishment collapsed, and seeing the police sent him into a state of madness. He was declared bankrupt, and the mere mention of money reduced him to a childlike state of embarrassment. One evening, while with Nana, Steiner began to cry and asked for a hundred francs to pay his servant. Nana, both sympathetic and amused, lent him the money, marking the end of the man who had pillaged the Paris market for two decades.

human_par3: The shocks of youthful minds among themselves have this admirable property, that one can never foresee the spark, nor divine the lightning flash. What will dart out presently? No one knows. The burst of laughter starts from a tender feeling. At the moment of jest, the serious makes its entry. Impulses depend on the first chance word. The spirit of each is sovereign, jest suffices to open the field to the unexpected. These are conversations with abrupt turns, in which the perspective changes suddenly. Chance is the stage-manager of such conversations.

THE JOSTLINGS of young minds against each other have this wonderful attribute, that one can never foresee the spark, nor predict the flash. What may spring up in a moment? Nobody knows. A burst of laughter follows a scene of tenderness. In a moment of buffoonery, the serious makes its entrance. Impulses depend upon a chance word. The spirit of each is sovereign. A jest suffices to open the door to the unlooked for. Theirs are conferences with sharp turns, where the perspective suddenly changes. Chance is the director of these conversations.

human_par3: “Now wait, just a moment. You may as well give this into the hands of the Emperor himself—else those blasted court poets will demand an audience with him and yap away forever. You may tell me the rest when you have given your message. Now, you—” and Chinna Pazhuvettarayar sought out one of the soldiers near him. “Escort this youngster to the Chakravarthy.”
llama_31_8b: "Let's discuss this later. You can take this letter to the Emperor right away. We can have a more in-depth conversation about it later, but for now, you can inform him yourself." He then instructed a nearby soldier to escort the young soldier to the Emperor's private chambers.

human_par3: ‘They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming, after them. I’m going open all the doors, and summon all my daughters, all of them, right now, right now,’ Lebedev whispered in fear, waving his arms and lunging from one door to the next.

“They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming after them. I’ll open all the doors and call all my daughters, everybody, now, right now,” Lebedev whispered fearfully, waving his arms and dashing from one door to the other.
llama_31_8b: Lebedev was agitated as he exclaimed, "They've arrived, they're here!" Rushing from room to room, he sharply urged, "The General is bringing up the rear. I'll throw open all the doors and summon all my daughters immediately."
gemini_25_flash: "They're on their way, here they come. The General is bringing up the back. I'll throw open every door and summon all my daughters, each and every one, immediately, this very moment," Lebedev softly stammered in a frenzy, rushing frantically between doors.
dipper_lowmed: “They’re coming, here they are. I’ll rush through all the doors and call my daughters, now, this minute,” Lebedev muttered softly in a panic, running from one door to another. “And the general will come at the last moment.”

human_par3: And—the torso of a goat-footed caryatid. Since such time as a carriage drawn by a pair of lathery black horses came hurtling up to the entrance-porch, since such time as a court footman in a loose-flowing cape with a tricorn askew on his head first threw open the lacquered, emblazoned orifice, and the door, with a click, cast aside a coat of arms (a unicorn, striking a knight) embellished with crowns; since such time as a statue with a face of parchment, rising from the coach’s funereal cushions, set foot upon the granite of the entrance-porch; since such time as a suede-gloved hand touched the rim of a top hat as it returned other people’s bows:—since such time a yet more mighty power had subdued the Establishment which cast its own mighty power all across Russia.

A footman, wearing a stylish hat, stepped forward and released the door handle, causing it to swing open. The door revealed a coat of arms on the door, decorated with small crowns and featuring a depiction of a unicorn attacking a knight. A gloved hand raised the brim of a tall, stylish hat in response to the greetings.

human_par3: “Thandavaraya, you'd better make sure this animal really is a horse, and not a false-legged prop, brought for the temple festivities,” mocked another soldier.

“Thandavaraya! Make sure that this horse, which refuses to allow you to mount it, is real. Maybe it’s a horse on stilts, come for the Perumal temple festival.” Thandavarayan, who had tried to mount the horse, said, “Let’s take a look,” and twisted the horse's tail.
llama_31_8b: One of the soldiers doubted the authenticity of the horse, suggesting that it might be part of a deceptive troupe that mimicks horse movements.
gemini_25_flash: "Thandavaraya," a soldier remarked, "I doubt this is a genuine horse. It likely belongs to a troupe that performs with prop horses." "Very well," came the reply.
dipper_lowmed: “Thandavaraya, this horse is not real. It seems to belong to the poikal kudiraiyar group,” said one of the soldiers.

human_par3: There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
llama_31_8b:
The emperor Taizong enters the temple, accompanied by his guards and officials, and is greeted by the vibrant atmosphere of the holy site. The streets are filled with intricately designed flags and banners that dance in the breeze, while the imposing presence of gold and jade statues of the Buddha and his followers command reverence. The emperor is deeply moved by the grandeur of the temple and the devotion of its priests.

As he enters the temple, Taizong is offered a grand procession of monks who have come to greet him. The Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, leads the procession and welcomes the emperor with a reverent bow. The emperor and his officials then participate in a special ceremony, where they offer incense and pay homage to the Buddha and his followers.

After the ceremony, the Master of the Law presents the emperor with a proclamation, which outlines the importance of Buddhism and the benefits of following the Buddhist path. The proclamation emphasizes the vast and endless nature of the universe and the importance of compassion and mercy. It invites the masses to participate in a grand Buddhist ceremony, which will offer them the opportunity to find release from suffering and achieve ultimate bliss.

Moved by the contents of the proclamation, the emperor exhorts the monks to remain dedicated to their devotion and service to the Buddha. He promises to reward them generously for their efforts and encourages them to continue their work without faltering in their dedication.
gemini_25_flash:
A vast procession led by countless nobles in ceremonial attire, flanked by guardsmen carrying an array of weapons, and punctuated by red silk lanterns and an ornate incense urn, moved with solemn grandeur. Mythical creatures seemed to dance and soar, reflecting the auspiciousness of the occasion. The emperor, described as a virtuous and wise ruler, surrounded by equally righteous ministers, was lauded for bringing prosperity and peace that surpassed legendary ancient leaders.

The spectacle continued with elaborately decorated umbrellas and robes emblazoned with dragons, their brilliance shimmering against each other. Jade rings and phoenix fans swayed through a mystical mist, while dignitaries displayed their status with pearl caps, jade belts, and golden medals. A thousand soldiers guarded the emperor, and two lines of marshals attended his carriage.

This devout emperor, pure of heart, bowed before the Buddha, eager to offer incense and cultivate virtue. The Tang emperor's grand procession soon reached the temple. He ordered the music to cease, dismounted from his carriage, and, accompanied by his officials, approached the Buddha's image with burning incense sticks. After three bows, they surveyed the magnificent religious hall.

The scene inside was breathtaking: vibrant flags and banners fluttered, creating shimmering, colorful mists, while radiant sunshades glowed like fiery bolts. An imposing golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats commanded attention. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a "brilliant forest of brocade," and sandalwood incense burned in urns, its fragrant smoke rising in "waves of fragrant clouds" that enveloped the clear sky. Red trays were laden with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters held mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras, seeking to bring solace to orphaned souls.

Taizong and his officials offered incense, worshipped the golden Buddha, and paid homage to the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, along with other monks, greeted the emperor. After the ceremony, everyone returned to their designated places according to their rank. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the salvation of orphaned souls.

The proclamation began by extolling the boundless and eternal nature of Buddhist virtue, rooted in Nirvāṇa, and the universal reach of purity throughout all realms. It spoke of countless transformations governed by yin and yang, and the vastness of phenomena's essence, function, true nature, and permanence. It then expressed deep pity for orphaned souls and announced that, by Taizong's decree, priests had been assembled to meditate and preach the Law. Their mission was to open the gates of salvation and provide countless means of mercy to deliver the multitudes from suffering, save them from perdition and the cycle of rebirth, and guide them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It urged them to embrace this opportunity to find release from hell, ascend to ultimate happiness, and travel freely to the Western Pure Land.

A poem within the proclamation spoke of an "urn of immortal incense" and "scrolls of salvific power," promising boundless divine grace, the annulment of guilt and crime, and the release of lost souls from their confinement. It concluded with a wish for the nation's firm and lasting peace.

Taizong, greatly pleased, addressed the monks, urging them to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha. He assured them that their efforts would be richly rewarded once they had achieved merit and received their blessings, promising that their labors would not be in vain.

This time she finished off Steiner, she sent him back to the pavement, sucked to the bone, so exhausted that he remained even incapable of inventing any new rogueries, a man who had known a million or two in his lifetime. One evening he burst into tears when she was with him; he asked her to lend him a hundred francs to pay his servant; and she brought him the money, saying, “You silly little Jew,” which she said to him, and it seemed to her as if she were gratifying an old hatred which she could not explain to herself.

There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.

Steiner was involved in a failing Bosphorus speculation, and Nana accelerated its demise by overspending lavishly. Despite his initial successes, his empire unraveled as investors from across Europe sought to recover their losses. Those from all walks of life - the wealthy as well as the poor - were left ruined, their savings and hard-earned earnings lost in Nana's extravagant lifestyle. She had also formed a partnership with an ironfounder in Alsace, where workers toiled tirelessly to generate funds for her indulgences. Nana left a trail of devastation as she consumed everything in her path, including stolen funds and the earnings of those who worked hard for their livelihood. Steiner was left a hollow shell, unable to come up with new schemes. His banking establishment collapsed, and seeing the police sent him into a state of madness. He was declared bankrupt, and the mere mention of money reduced him to a childlike state of embarrassment. One evening, while with Nana, Steiner began to cry and asked for a hundred francs to pay his servant. Nana, both sympathetic and amused, lent him the money, marking the end of the man who had pillaged the Paris market for two decades.

When young minds meet, the unpredictable nature of their interaction is truly remarkable. There's no telling what kind of excitement or new ideas will emerge. What happens next is always a surprise. Laughter often follows moments of understanding, and serious thoughts can suddenly appear in a lighthearted exchange. A single word can trigger new impulses. The energy and spirit of each person are key. Even a simple joke can open the door to unforeseen developments. These conversations are full of abrupt shifts and sudden changes in viewpoint. Chance encounters and fortunate coincidences are what drive these discussions forward.

"Hold on! Let's discuss this another time. You can take this message directly to the Emperor now. When the poets arrive, they can continue their conversation then." "Take this young soldier to the Emperor's presence," he instructed one of the nearby guards.

“They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming after them. I’ll open all the doors and call all my daughters, everybody, now, right now,” Lebedev whispered fearfully, waving his arms and dashing from one door to the other.

A caryatid with a goat-like lower body stood prominently. A pair of horses pulled a carriage swiftly to the entrance, where a footman, wearing a three-cornered hat, clicked the door handle. The door swung open, revealing a coat of arms featuring small crowns and depicting a unicorn impaling a knight. In response to greetings, a gloved hand briefly touched the brim of a top hat.
"Thandavaraya," a soldier remarked, "I doubt this is a genuine horse. It likely belongs to a troupe that performs with prop horses." "Very well," came the reply.

A vast procession led by countless nobles in ceremonial attire, flanked by guardsmen carrying an array of weapons, and punctuated by red silk lanterns and an ornate incense urn, moved with solemn grandeur. Mythical creatures seemed to dance and soar, reflecting the auspiciousness of the occasion. The emperor, described as a virtuous and wise ruler, surrounded by equally righteous ministers, was lauded for bringing prosperity and peace that surpassed legendary ancient leaders.

The spectacle continued with elaborately decorated umbrellas and robes emblazoned with dragons, their brilliance shimmering against each other. Jade rings and phoenix fans swayed through a mystical mist, while dignitaries displayed their status with pearl caps, jade belts, and golden medals. A thousand soldiers guarded the emperor, and two lines of marshals attended his carriage.

This devout emperor, pure of heart, bowed before the Buddha, eager to offer incense and cultivate virtue. The Tang emperor's grand procession soon reached the temple. He ordered the music to cease, dismounted from his carriage, and, accompanied by his officials, approached the Buddha's image with burning incense sticks. After three bows, they surveyed the magnificent religious hall.

The scene inside was breathtaking: vibrant flags and banners fluttered, creating shimmering, colorful mists, while radiant sunshades glowed like fiery bolts. An imposing golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats commanded attention. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a "brilliant forest of brocade," and sandalwood incense burned in urns, its fragrant smoke rising in "waves of fragrant clouds" that enveloped the clear sky. Red trays were laden with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters held mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras, seeking to bring solace to orphaned souls.

Taizong and his officials offered incense, worshipped the golden Buddha, and paid homage to the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, along with other monks, greeted the emperor. After the ceremony, everyone returned to their designated places according to their rank. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the salvation of orphaned souls.

The proclamation began by extolling the boundless and eternal nature of Buddhist virtue, rooted in Nirvāṇa, and the universal reach of purity throughout all realms. It spoke of countless transformations governed by yin and yang, and the vastness of phenomena's essence, function, true nature, and permanence. It then expressed deep pity for orphaned souls and announced that, by Taizong's decree, priests had been assembled to meditate and preach the Law. Their mission was to open the gates of salvation and provide countless means of mercy to deliver the multitudes from suffering, save them from perdition and the cycle of rebirth, and guide them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It urged them to embrace this opportunity to find release from hell, ascend to ultimate happiness, and travel freely to the Western Pure Land.

A poem within the proclamation spoke of an "urn of immortal incense" and "scrolls of salvific power," promising boundless divine grace, the annulment of guilt and crime, and the release of lost souls from their confinement. It concluded with a wish for the nation's firm and lasting peace.

Taizong, greatly pleased, addressed the monks, urging them to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha. He assured them that their efforts would be richly rewarded once they had achieved merit and received their blessings, promising that their labors would not be in vain.

He told them. But we shall not tell it here. He told them, briefly and impersonally; he did not talk about love and feelings; he only said that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself, in view of certain new developments; he did not bother to explain the reasons for his actions, he did not go into subtleties; besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was evident that it was not this that interested them most.

All six tiny rooms were heated by steam, for which reason you were suffocated in the little flat by a damp, greenhouse heat; the panes of the windows sweated; Sofia Petrovna’s visitor sweated; the chambermaid and the husband sweated endlessly; and Sofia Petrovna herself was covered in perspiration like a Japanese chrysanthemum in warm dew. So where would any perspective come from in such a hothouse?

"Oh!" he blurted out, cutting himself off. "Here come Mignon and Steiner! They're inseparable. You know Steiner is getting tired of Rose, so her husband is clinging to him like glue to prevent him from leaving."
